FT_IC1.evidence: Data from U.S. ABCD Study, data release 5.0, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, children enrolled ages 9-10 years; N=8324.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using linear mixed-effects models and cross-lagged panel models over 4 annual waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics Open Science; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media use (texting + social networking sites + video chat per YSTS, items 4+5+6) analyzed separately with its own coefficient in Table 2 (social media use x time: β=0.03 [SE 0.01], P<.001; social media use x time x PGS-ADHD: β=-0.04 [0.01]; cumulative β=0.15 [0.03]), paired with inattention and combined ADHD symptoms (individual-trait outcome).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported in Table 2 for social media x time interactions; cross-lagged path coefficients in Figure 3 (γ=0.03, P<.01 at each wave); cumulative effect β=0.15.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported throughout: Table 2 standardized betas with SEs, cross-lagged panel coefficients, cumulative effect estimates.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is IV (predicting ADHD/inattention symptoms as DV) in the primary longitudinal models; EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: Social media use composite = texting + social networking + video chat (YSTS items 4+5+6), distinct from video games and TV/videos which are analyzed as separate categories. PGS-ADHD included as covariate/moderator.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from U.S. ABCD Study, data release 5.1, 21 U.S. sites, children recruited ages 9-10; analysis subsample N=1940 at ages 11-12 at Wave 1.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using modified Poisson regression and latent class trajectory modeling over three annual waves (Waves 1-3 of ABCD follow-up).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Obesity (Silver Spring); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media addiction (SMA) scale (6-item Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale adapted) is a qualifying SM variable (problematic/addictive SM scale); it has its own coefficient as IV paired with binge eating symptoms (psychiatric/behavioral health outcome, individual-trait level). Table 3: addiction SMA group vs. no symptoms, prospective RR=1.90 (95% CI: 1.10-3.27); Table 2 cross-sectional PR=2.17 (95% CI: 1.22-3.84). Time on social media (SMT) also separately analyzed with its own coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: PRs and RRs with 95% CIs reported in Tables 2, 3, and 4 for SMA and SMT associations with binge eating; trajectory group RRs in Table 4 (consistently high SMA: RR=3.01 [95% CI: 2.07-4.37]).

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: prevalence ratios (Table 2), relative risks (Table 3), trajectory-based RRs (Table 4), stratified analyses in supplementary tables.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media (SMA and SMT) is IV predicting binge eating as DV; SM is not DV in any analysis. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis.
notes: SMA is the Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale adapted — a qualifying problematic SM scale used as IV, not treated as individual-trait outcome. BE (binge eating) is the individual-trait outcome. Both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses present.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from U.S. ABCD Study, data release 5.0, 21 U.S. sites, children initially aged 9-10 years; N=10,116 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using cross-sectional and longitudinal linear mixed-effects models and mediation analyses on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Translational Psychiatry; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper (main tables S2/S3, results text). Screen time is analyzed only as a single undifferentiated composite. Step 2: Methods explicitly state screen time was 'calculated as the total time spent using various devices, including playing video games and watching television' — the composite bundles TV and video games with any other device use; no separate breakout of social media, phone time, or texting exists anywhere in the paper. Fails Step 2 because non-phone/non-SM activities (TV, video games) are bundled in without a separate breakout.

notes: Screen time composite explicitly includes video games and TV watching per methods section; no decomposition into SM-specific or phone-specific components anywhere in the paper.
